summary: Joel documents the challenges of finding office space in New York City that provides both private offices and natural daylight. He explains the geometry problem: the distance from outer windows to the building core determines how many offices can receive light, with roughly 30 feet being the maximum usable depth. Manhattan's building stock is optimized for large open floorplates suited to trading floors and cubicle farms, making private offices with daylight nearly impossible to find.
key_points: Private offices with natural daylight require the outer wall-to-core distance to be 30 feet or less, limiting viable buildings
Manhattan's standard block dimensions (264 feet apart) produce buildings with 60-70 foot depths, double what works for private offices
Wall Street's demand for large unobstructed trading floors has driven NYC developers to optimize for exactly the opposite of what software teams need
Smaller buildings that don't fill a block often lack windows on all sides, pushing the core to windowless walls and worsening the depth problem
money_quotes: Our desire to have (a) private offices with (b) lots of daylight is very hard to accomodate in New York City.
If the distance from the outside window to the core is more than, say, 30 feet, you either have to waste space or give someone a dark office.
Those big office buildings you see that take up an entire city block might have floorplans that are 200 x 200 feet.
If you're building a sea of cubicles, this is very efficient. If you want 100% private offices, this doesn't work well at all.
The ideal trading floor is a huge square, the bigger the better, without a single column. This is exactly the opposite of what software developers want.
key_insight: Manhattan's commercial real estate is geometrically optimized for open floorplans and trading floors, making it structurally hostile to the private-office-with-daylight layout that benefits software developers.
